pattern: Every node has exactly one outgoing edge, so following edges from any start eventually loops onto a cycle; walking each unexplored chain once, detecting where it closes a loop or merges into an already-solved chain, and propagating counts backward answers every node in linear time.
hints: Since every node points to exactly one other node, starting anywhere and repeatedly following edges traces out a single chain that must eventually revisit some node.

Walk from an unvisited node keeping the ordered list of nodes on the current walk; if you land back on a node from that same walk, everything from that node onward forms the cycle, and everything before it is a tail leading into it.

Once a node's answer is known (it is part of a finished cycle or an already-resolved tail), any node whose walk merges into it gets an answer of one plus that known value, propagated backward along the walk.

edge_inputs.description: the smallest possible graph, a two-node cycle
a plain three-node cycle with no tails
a single tail node feeding into a two-node cycle
two separate two-node cycles with no connection between them
a chain of tail nodes feeding into a three-node cycle
several independent tails all merging into the same two-node cycle
a longer tail before reaching a cycle, so the walk revisits a node only after several extra hops

stress.description: a large random functional graph with no self-loops
one giant cycle spanning every node, the worst case for cycle detection
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    edges = [(i + 1) % n for i in range(n)]
    return [edges]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def countVisitedNodes(self, edges):
        n = len(edges)
        ans = []
        for start in range(n):
            seen_order = []
            seen_set = set()
            node = start
            while node not in seen_set:
                seen_set.add(node)
                seen_order.append(node)
                node = edges[node]
            ans.append(len(seen_order))
        return ans
